parts.content: Discuss how the PETTLEP model can be used to structure imagery training for athletes returning from injury.
parts.markscheme:
- Physical: Athletes should adopt the same posture, clothing, and equipment as during performance to increase realism. <marks>A1</marks>
- Environment: Imagery should be conducted in the actual or a similar performance setting to increase contextual relevance. <marks>A1</marks>
- Task: The content of the imagery should match the specific technical and tactical demands of the sport-specific task. <marks>A1</marks>
- Timing: Imagery should be performed in real-time to reinforce the correct rhythm and duration of the movement. <marks>A1</marks>
- Learning: Imagery scripts should be updated to reflect the athlete's rehabilitation progress and skill development. <marks>A1</marks>
- Emotion: Athletes should incorporate the emotions they expect to feel (e.g., nerves, confidence) to simulate the competitive experience. <marks>A1</marks>
- Perspective: Imagery can be performed from an internal or external view depending on which is most vivid and effective for the athlete. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the role of imagery in the development of confidence and mental toughness in elite athletes. Give examples of elite athletes who have used imagery to develop these qualities.
parts.markscheme:
### Role of imagery (confidence and mental toughness)
- Imagery can enhance confidence by rehearsing successful performance (mastery imagery), increasing perceived competence/self-efficacy and positive outcome expectations. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Imagery can support mental toughness by rehearsing coping with pressure/adversity (coping/stress-inoculation imagery), improving focus and emotional control under stress. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Imagery can be structured using PETTLEP principles (physical, environment, task, timing, learning, emotion, perspective) to increase functional equivalence and effectiveness. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Imagery is one component of psychological skills training; benefits are typically greatest when combined with physical practice and other skills (eg goal setting, self-talk, arousal regulation). <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Examples (credit any valid, named elite athlete example linked to imagery)
- Example such as Michael Phelps using race visualisation/mental rehearsal to prepare for competition and maintain confidence under pressure. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Example such as Tiger Woods using visualisation to rehearse shots and manage competitive pressure. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Critical/balanced discussion (additional acceptable points)
- Effectiveness can depend on imagery ability (vividness/controllability) and the athlete’s experience level. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Overuse of outcome imagery without process detail may increase anxiety or unrealistic expectations; process/mastery imagery is often more effective for confidence. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain the use of healing imagery in the context of sports injuries, including how athletes can visualise recovery and its potential influence on the healing process.
parts.markscheme:
### Healing imagery: what it involves
- Athlete visualises the injured area healing (eg reduced swelling, tissue repair, improved blood flow) and/or visualises completing rehab exercises correctly and progressively. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use PETTLEP to make rehabilitation imagery more realistic (eg imaging in the rehab environment, matching timing, including emotions/pain sensations appropriately).  <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Potential influence (psychological and indirect physiological pathways)
- Can reduce pain perception and anxiety, improve mood, and increase perceived control/self-efficacy during rehabilitation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improved confidence and motivation may increase adherence to rehabilitation and quality of movement practice, indirectly supporting return to play. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Evidence for direct acceleration of tissue healing is mixed; credit responses that qualify claims and focus on likely psychological/behavioural benefits. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Additional acceptable points
- Can be combined with relaxation to down-regulate arousal and manage stress during rehab sessions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May help maintain technical/skill representations during time away from practice, supporting smoother return to performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate the limitations of using imagery for performance enhancement in sports, considering individual differences, training experience, and potential over-reliance on mental techniques.
parts.markscheme:
### Limitations of using imagery (evaluation)
- Individual differences: athletes vary in imagery ability (vividness/controllability) and may find imagery difficult or distracting. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Training experience: novices may lack accurate movement representations, reducing the effectiveness/accuracy of imagery compared with more experienced athletes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Over-reliance: imagery cannot replace physical practice; excessive focus on mental rehearsal may reduce time/quality of physical training and feedback. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Poorly designed imagery (eg unrealistic outcome-only imagery, wrong perspective, mismatched timing) may increase anxiety or reinforce errors. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Additional acceptable points
- Context dependence: benefits may be smaller under fatigue/distraction or when competition conditions differ from the imaged environment. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Requires consistent practice and guidance; without structure (eg PETTLEP) effects may be limited. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe how the coach could implement imagery in training and before competition for this team, and outline potential outcomes.
parts.markscheme:
### Implementation and techniques
- Provide structured imagery sessions (team and/or individual) where athletes mentally rehearse successful skill execution (e.g., passes, shots, defensive rotations). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use a script that includes vivid multi-sensory cues (visual, kinaesthetic, auditory) and emotional control cues (e.g., calm breathing while imagining pressure moments). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Schedule imagery at specific times (e.g., pre-training warm-up, pre-game routine, or post-training review) and set clear goals/targets for each session (specific scenario or set play). <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Outcomes
- Outcomes may include improved confidence/self-efficacy, improved focus/attention control, reduced competitive anxiety, and/or better execution of tactics under pressure (which may also support cohesion). <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how the use of imagery might vary between an individual sport (e.g., swimming) and a team sport (e.g., basketball/football), and how this can impact performance.
parts.markscheme:
### Individual sports
- Imagery is often centred on personal technique, pacing, and self-regulation (internal perspective), e.g., a swimmer visualizing stroke mechanics and turns. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Imagery may emphasize controllable performance processes (rhythm, breathing patterns, race plan) and individual arousal control strategies. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Team sports
- Imagery often integrates tactical decision-making, coordination and timing with teammates (external or mixed perspective), e.g., imagining a set play and reading opponents’ cues. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- May include communication and role clarity (where to move, who to cover) to support collective organization. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Impact on performance
- Individual-sport imagery primarily enhances personal skill consistency and confidence, whereas team-sport imagery can enhance tactical execution and shared understanding/cohesion; both can improve performance via better focus and reduced errors under pressure. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Analyse how an athlete might use imagery to overcome pre-competition anxiety and improve focus, considering both emotional and cognitive aspects.
parts.markscheme:
### Emotional aspects
- Use calming/positive imagery to replace threat appraisals with feelings of control (e.g., imagining staying calm during a hostile crowd or after a mistake). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Visualize successful coping and positive outcomes to increase confidence and reduce worry (e.g., seeing oneself executing the first play successfully). <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Cognitive aspects
- Mentally rehearse a clear performance plan (key cues, routines, and responses to likely situations), increasing predictability and perceived control. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use imagery as attentional control: focusing on task-relevant cues (e.g., “hands up on defence”, “follow-through”) and screening out distractions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Outcome
- Reduced cognitive and somatic anxiety symptoms and improved concentration/focus during performance (fewer attentional lapses, better decision-making). <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A study examined the effect of imagery on free-throw accuracy in elite basketball players. Athletes were assigned to one of three groups:

*   Motivational General Mastery (MG-M) imagery group
*   Cognitive Specific (CS) imagery group
*   Control group with no imagery intervention

Each group followed a 3-week training protocol. Imagery sessions for both experimental groups followed the PETTLEP model, aiming to increase functional equivalence with real performance. The outcome was free-throw accuracy (% of successful shots) and self-rated imagery vividness on a $1\text{--}10$ scale post-intervention.

**Table 1: Free-Throw Accuracy ($\text{\%} \pm \text{SD}$)**

| Group   | Pre (%)          | Post (%)         |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| MG-M    | $68.2 \pm 6.1$   | $78.5 \pm 5.4$   |
| CS      | $69.0 \pm 5.7$   | $75.2 \pm 6.0$   |
| Control | $67.5 \pm 6.4$   | $67.9 \pm 6.2$   |
  
**Figure 1: Imagery Vividness Rating ($1\text{--}10$ scale)**

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/f24b42c9-8191-4f7f-b844-05e36a460b03.png)

questions.specification:
A coach works with an athlete during a 6-week pre-season phase. The athlete’s main long-term aim is to improve 5 km running performance from 22 min 00 s to 20 min 30 s by the end of week 6.

Table 1 shows weekly 5 km time-trial results and the athlete’s confidence rating (0–10).

| Week | 5 km time-trial (min:s) | Confidence rating (0–10) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 22:00 | 5 |
| 2 | 21:45 | 5 |
| 3 | 21:35 | 6 |
| 4 | 21:20 | 6 |
| 5 | 21:05 | 7 |
| 6 | 20:50 | 8 |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.1 Goal setting > C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.1.1 Goal setting as a tool for enhancing motivation through outcome and performance goals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on defining goal setting, types of goals (e.g., outcome/performance/process), links to motivation and confidence, SMART goals, and coach support—core content of goal setting as a psychological skill.

parts.content: Define goal setting in the context of sports.
parts.markscheme:
- Goal setting is the process of establishing objectives/targets for performance/training in sport. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Objectives are typically clear/specific and can be monitored/evaluated (for example measurable and time-bound). <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other appropriate responses such as reference to goals providing direction/standards for performance.</note>

parts.content: State two types of goals athletes may set.
parts.markscheme:
- Outcome goals. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Performance goals. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other appropriate responses such as process goals, short-term goals, or long-term goals.</note>

parts.content: Outline three reasons why goal setting can enhance motivation.
parts.markscheme:
- Provides a clear focus and sense of purpose (clarifies what to work towards). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Encourages persistence/commitment by providing small, achievable targets. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provides a benchmark for progress and improvement, reinforcing training effort. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other appropriate responses such as improving planning/prioritization or time management.</note>

parts.content: Explain, using Table 1, how goal setting could influence the athlete’s confidence.
parts.markscheme:
- As performance improves across weeks (5 km time decreases from 22:00 to 20:50), successful attainment of targets can increase self-efficacy/self-belief. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Confidence rating increases from 5 (week 1) to 8 (week 6), consistent with confidence being reinforced by evidence of competence/progress. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Short-term/weekly goals (for example reducing time by small increments) can make improvement feel achievable, reducing fear of failure and promoting controllable focus. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reaching milestones (for example sub-21:00 by week 6 is approached at 20:50) can strengthen positive expectations for future performance. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other appropriate responses such as links to reduced anxiety/nerves or increased positive self-talk, provided they are linked to goal attainment/progress in Table 1.</note>

parts.content: Discuss how SMART goals could be used for this athlete’s training and performance across the 6 weeks.
parts.markscheme:
- Specific: define a clear target (for example reduce 5 km time) rather than a vague aim. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Measurable: use time-trial times/confidence ratings to track progress objectively each week. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Achievable/Realistic: set weekly targets aligned to current performance so goals are challenging but attainable. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Time-bound/Relevant: set deadlines within the 6-week period that align with the athlete’s long-term aim and training plan. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other appropriate responses such as accountability, sustained motivation, improved focus, and reduced distractions, provided they relate to applying the SMART framework to the scenario.</note>

parts.content: Explain how a coach might help this athlete implement effective goal-setting strategies.
parts.markscheme:
- Break the long-term goal into short-term process/performance goals for each week/session. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ensure goals are individualized and at an appropriate level of challenge (adjust if too easy/too difficult). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Monitor progress regularly (for example review weekly time-trial data) and revise goals when needed. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provide feedback and reinforcement (for example acknowledge improvements/milestones) to maintain motivation and adherence. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Accept other appropriate responses such as encouraging athlete autonomy in setting goals, using training logs, or linking imagery/visualization to goal attainment.</note>

questions.specification: A skier mentally rehearses the feeling of carving through the snow before a race. Which psychological technique is this an example of?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C.5 Psychological skills > C.5.2 Imagery > C.5.2.1 Imagery mimics real experience using sensory modalities to improve performance (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.2.1 Imagery mimics real experience using sensory modalities to improve performance (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The scenario describes mentally rehearsing the feeling of performing a skill (carving through snow), which is the use of imagery using sensory modalities to enhance performance.

options.content: Goal setting
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Goal setting involves establishing specific performance or outcome objectives rather than mentally rehearsing sensory experiences.

options.content: Imagery
options.markscheme: Correct. Imagery (mental rehearsal/visualization) involves creating or recreating experiences in the mind, including kinaesthetic feelings such as carving through snow.

options.content: Attribution theory
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Attribution theory concerns how performers explain causes of success and failure (e.g., internal/external, stable/unstable), not mental rehearsal.

options.content: Feedback
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Feedback is information about performance (intrinsic or extrinsic) used to improve future performance, not a pre-performance mental rehearsal technique.

questions.specification: Which combination of sensory modalities is generally considered most effective for performance imagery in sport?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: C.5.2.1 Imagery mimics real experience using sensory modalities to improve performance (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which sensory modalities (vision, kinaesthetic feel, auditory, etc.) are most effective in imagery to enhance sport performance, which directly matches the imagery topic about using sensory modalities to mimic real experience.

options.content: Visual only
options.markscheme: Imagery effectiveness is increased when multiple senses are engaged rather than only one.

options.content: Visual and kinaesthetic
options.markscheme: A multisensory approach is preferred; adding auditory cues is generally considered more effective than excluding them.

options.content: Visual, kinaesthetic, and auditory
options.markscheme: This combination reflects a multisensory approach to imagery, commonly recommended as most effective for enhancing sport performance.

options.content: Auditory only
options.markscheme: Using only auditory cues is generally less effective than combining key modalities such as visual and kinaesthetic.

options.content: Improves skill retention and confidence
options.markscheme: Correct: mental imagery helps maintain neural representations of the skill and can support confidence during injury lay-off.

options.markscheme: Incorrect: imagery does not produce the mechanical overload required to significantly increase strength/power.

options.markscheme: Incorrect: imagery does not directly increase physiological tissue healing rates such as bone repair.
